en.extract:
Hellmayr's pipit (Anthus hellmayri) is a species of bird in the family Motacillidae, the wagtails and pipits. It is found in Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Chile, Paraguay, Peru, and Uruguay.


Taxonomy and systematics
Hellmayr's pipit was described by Ernst Hartert in 1909 with its current binomial Anthus hellmayri. Hartert's specific epithet hellmayri honors Austrian ornithologist Carl Eduard Hellmayr; its English name came later.
Hellmayr's pipit has these three subspecies:

A. h. hellmayri (Hartert, EJO, 1909)
A. h. dabbenei (Hellmayr, 1921)
A. h. brasilianus (Hellmayr, 1921)
Some authors suggest that genetic differences among the subspecies are large enough that two full species may be present.


Description
Hellmayr's pipit is about 14 to 16 cm (5.5 to 6.3 in) long. Subspecies A. h. brasilianus weighs 16.5 to 20 g (0.58 to 0.71 oz) The sexes have the same plumage. Adults of the nominate subspecies A. h. hellmayri have a pale buff head with a paler area around the eye. Their upperparts are pale buff with bold blackish brown streaks. Their wings are dusky with whitish edges on the primaries and tertials and buff edges on the secondaries. Their wing coverts have buff edges that show as two wing bars. Their tail is mostly dusky with mostly buffy white outermost feathers. Their throat and underparts are buffy white with thin dusky streaks on the breast and flanks. Subspecies A. h. dabbenei is almost identical to the nominate but has a white spot near the end of the next-to-outermost pair of tail feathers. A. h. brasilianus has cinnamon edges on all of its wing feathers and its underparts have a cinnamon tinge. All subspecies have a dark brown iris, a blackish maxilla, a pale pinkish to yellowish mandible with a dark tip, and bright pink legs and feet.


Distribution and habitat
Hellmayr's pipit has a highly disjunct distribution. The subspecies are found thus:

A. h. hellmayri: eastern slope of the Andes from Puno Department in far southeastern Peru south through Bolivia into northwestern Argentina to La Rioja Province; separately in Córdoba Province; one record in Peru's Ayacucho Department
A. h. dabbenei: Argentina from Córdoba and Entre Ríos provinces south to Santa Cruz Province and in adjoining eastern Chile; separately in Argentina on the Somuncurá Plateau in southeastern Río Negro Province
A. h. brasilianus: southeastern Brazil from southern Espírito Santo and southern Minas Gerais south; southeastern Paraguay; from Paraguay south across northeastern Argentina's Corrientes Province into far southern Brazil; from southern Brazil south though eastern Uruguay into northeastern Argentina to eastern Buenos Aires Province
Hellmayr's pipit inhabits a wide variety of open landscapes. In the western part of its range it favors moist puna grasslands. In the east it is more of a generalist, occurring in Pampas grasslands, rocky hillsides, pastures, and agricultural areas. Everywhere it favors short grass or short herbaceous foliage. The nominate subspecies occurs between 2,450 and 3,350 m (8,000 and 11,000 ft) in Peru and reaches 3,700 m (12,100 ft) elsewhere. Subspecies A. h. dabbenei reaches 3,600 m (11,800 ft). A. h. brasilianus is mostly found from sea level to 750 m (2,500 ft) but locally reaches 2,200 m (7,200 ft) in Brazil.


Behavior


Movement
Hellmayr's pipit is a partial migrant. The nominate subspecies and A. h. brasilianus are year-round residents. Subspecies A. h. dabbenei vacates the area south from Argentina's Neuquén Province and adjoining Chile during the austral winter, moving north as far as Córdoba and Entre Ríos provinces in north-central Argentina. (Note that the map does not show this winter range.)


Feeding
The diet of Hellmayr's pipit has not been studied but is known to include larval and adult insects. It forages on the ground while walking or running.


Breeding
The breeding season of Hellmayr's pipit varies geographically. In Brazil it spans at least September to April. In Bolivia it includes November to January, in Argentina September to February, and in Chile from September to January. Males make a flight display that can reach 60 m (200 ft) above the ground with a spiral descent to the ground. At least in Argentina, both sexes build the nest. The nest is a domed cup made mostly of grass on the ground or attached to low vegetation. The clutch is three to five eggs that are grayish to creamy white with brown or olive-brown spots. In a study in Argentina incubation lasted 13 to 14 days and fledging occurred nine to 14 days after hatch. Both parents provision nestlings.


Vocalization
Hellmayr's pipit sings from a perch, such as a shrub or fence post, and during the display flight. In Brazil its song is a "very/extr. high, short, hurried twitter ending in a nasal drawn-out note, together as tritfitwitweét-trutfutwuit-srèèh, given in 2-3 sec intervals". A study in Chile's Arauco Province found its flight song there is "a relatively fast sequence of 3–7 short, complex, high-pitched notes interspersed with a short buzz that becomes longer at the end of the song". When perched it sings a shorter song with shorter buzzes and sometimes just "high-pitched complex warbling notes".


Status
The IUCN has assessed Hellmayr's pipit as being of Least Concern. It has a very large range; its population size is not known and is believed to be stable. No immediate threats have been identified. It is "[l]ocally uncommon to frequent as a breeding species". However, its status in Argentina and Chile is not well understood. "Habitat fragmentation and land cover change are the main threats to this species."


References

'''Hellmayr's pipit''' ('''''Anthus hellmayri''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Motacillidae]], the wagtails and pipits. It is found in [[Argentina]], [[Bolivia]], [[Brazil]], [[Chile]], [[Paraguay]], [[Peru]], and [[Uruguay]].<ref name=IOC15.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/waxbills/ |title=Waxbills, parrotfinches, munias, whydahs, Olive Warbler, accentors, pipits |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 15.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=March 2025 |access-date=6 March 2026 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

Hellmayr's pipit was described by [[Ernst Hartert]] in 1909 with its current [[Binomial nomenclature|binomial]] ''Anthus hellmayri''. Hartert's [[specific epithet]] ''hellmayri'' honors Austrian ornithologist [[Carl Eduard Hellmayr]]; its English name came later.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Hartert |first=Ernst | journal=Novitates Zoologicae: A Journal of Zoology in Connection with the Tring Museum | title=Notes sur les oiseaux de la Republique Argentine | volume=XVI | pages=165 | publisher=Zoological Museum, Tring | date= 1909 |language=French | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/2996059 |access-date=March 9, 2026 }}</ref>

Hellmayr's pipit has these three subspecies:<ref name=IOC15.1/>

* ''A. h. hellmayri'' <small>([[Ernst Hartert|Hartert, EJO]], 1909)</small>
* ''A. h. dabbenei'' <small>([[Carl Eduard Hellmayr|Hellmayr]], 1921)</small>
* ''A. h. brasilianus'' <small>([[Carl Eduard Hellmayr|Hellmayr]], 1921)</small>

Some authors suggest that genetic differences among the subspecies are large enough that two full species may be present.<ref name=HEPI-BOW>Norambuena, H. V., M. A. Colombo, F. Rivas Fuenzalida, S. Tyler, and E. de Juana (2024). Hellmayr's Pipit (''Anthus hellmayri''), version 3.0. In Birds of the World (S. M. Billerman, F. Medrano, and T. S. Schulenberg, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.helpip1.03 retrieved March 9. 2026</ref>

==Description==

Hellmayr's pipit is about {{convert|14|to|16|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long. Subspecies ''A. h. brasilianus'' weighs {{convert|16.5|to|20|g|oz|2|abbr=on}} The sexes have the same plumage. Adults of the [[nominate subspecies]] ''A. h. hellmayri'' have a pale buff head with a paler area around the eye. Their upperparts are pale buff with bold blackish brown streaks. Their wings are dusky with whitish edges on the [[Primaries (birds)|primaries]] and [[tertials]] and buff edges on the [[Secondaries (birds)|secondaries]]. Their wing [[coverts]] have buff edges that show as two [[wing bar]]s. Their tail is mostly dusky with mostly buffy white outermost feathers. Their throat and underparts are buffy white with thin dusky streaks on the breast and flanks. Subspecies ''A. h. dabbenei'' is almost identical to the nominate but has a white spot near the end of the next-to-outermost pair of tail feathers. ''A. h. brasilianus'' has cinnamon edges on all of its wing feathers and its underparts have a cinnamon tinge. All subspecies have a dark brown iris, a blackish [[maxilla]], a pale pinkish to yellowish [[mandible]] with a dark tip, and bright pink legs and feet.<ref name=HEPI-BOW/>

==Distribution and habitat==

Hellmayr's pipit has a highly [[disjunct distribution]]. The subspecies are found thus:<ref name=HEPI-BOW/>

* ''A. h. hellmayri'': eastern slope of the Andes from [[Puno Department]] in far southeastern Peru<ref name=Schulenberg>{{cite book | last1 =Schulenberg | first1 =T.S. | last2 =Stotz | first2 =D.F. | last3 =Lane | first3 =D.F. | last4 =O'Neill | first4 =J.P. | last5 =Parker | first5 =T.A. III | title =Birds of Peru | publisher =Princeton University Press | edition =revised and updated |series=Princeton Field Guides | date =2010 | location =Princeton, NJ | pages =518  |isbn = 978-0691130231 }}</ref> south through Bolivia into northwestern Argentina to [[La Rioja Province, Argentina|La Rioja Province]]; separately in [[Córdoba Province, Argentina|Córdoba Province]]; one record in Peru's [[Ayacucho Department]]<ref>Hosner, P. A., M. J. Andersen, M. B. Robbins, A. Urbay-Tello, L. Cueto-Aparicio, K. Verde-Guerra, L. A. Sánchez-González, A. G. Navarro-Sigüenza, R. L. Boyd, J. Núñez, J. Tiravanti, M. Combe, H. L. Owens, and A. T. Peterson (2015). "Avifaunal surveys of the upper Apurímac River Valley, Ayacucho and Cuzco Departments, Peru. New distributional records and biogeographic, taxonomic, and conservation implications"''. The Wilson Journal of Ornithology'' 127:563–581.</ref> 
* ''A. h. dabbenei'': Argentina from [[Córdoba Province, Argentina|Córdoba]] and [[Entre Ríos Province|Entre Ríos]] provinces south to [[Santa Cruz Province, Argentina|Santa Cruz Province]] and in adjoining eastern Chile; separately in Argentina on the [[Somuncurá Provincial Reserve|Somuncurá Plateau]] in southeastern [[Río Negro Province]]
* ''A. h. brasilianus'': southeastern Brazil from southern [[Espírito Santo]] and southern [[Minas Gerais]] south<ref name=vanPerlo>{{cite book | last =van Perlo | first = Ber| title =A Field Guide to the Birds of Brazil | publisher =Oxford University Press | date =2009 | location =New York | pages =284–285 | isbn =978-0-19-530155-7 }}</ref><ref name=Vasconcelos>{{cite journal |last1=Ferreira de Vasconcelos|first1=Marcelo|last2=Esteves Lopes|first2=Leonardo|title=On the wide occurrence of the Hellmayr's pipit ''Anthus hellmayri'' in the espinhaço range, Southeastern Brazil, with comments on its natural history |journal=Interciencia |date=October 2011 |volume=36 |issue=10 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/237033132 |access-date=10 May 2024}}</ref>; southeastern Paraguay<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lowen |first1=James C. |last2=Clay |first2=Robert P. |last3=Barnett |first3=Juan Mazar |last4=N. |first4=Alberto Madroño |last5=Pearman |first5=Mark |last6=Lanús |first6=Bernabé López |last7=Tobias |first7=Joe A. |last8=Liley |first8=Durwyn C. |last9=Brooks |first9=Thomas M. |last10=Esquivel |first10=Estela Z. |last11=Reid |first11=Jane M. |title=New and noteworthy observations on the Paraguayan avifauna |journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club |date=1997 |volume=117 |issue=4 |page=278 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40058423#page/278/mode/1up |access-date=10 May 2024}}</ref>; from Paraguay south across northeastern Argentina's [[Corrientes Province]] into far southern Brazil; from southern Brazil south though eastern Uruguay into northeastern Argentina to eastern [[Buenos Aires Province]]

Hellmayr's pipit inhabits a wide variety of open landscapes. In the western part of its range it favors moist [[puna grassland]]s. In the east it is more of a generalist, occurring in [[Pampas]] grasslands, rocky hillsides, pastures, and agricultural areas. Everywhere it favors short grass or short [[herbaceous]] foliage.<ref name=HEPI-BOW/> The nominate subspecies occurs between {{convert|2450|and|3350|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}} in Peru<ref name=Schulenberg/> and reaches {{convert|3700|m|ft|abbr=on}} elsewhere<ref name=HEPI-BOW/>. Subspecies ''A. h. dabbenei'' reaches {{convert|3600|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=HEPI-BOW/> ''A. h. brasilianus'' is mostly found from sea level to {{convert|750|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}}<ref name=HEPI-BOW/> but locally reaches {{convert|2200|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Brazil<ref name=vanPerlo/>.

==Behavior==
===Movement===

Hellmayr's pipit is a partial migrant. The nominate subspecies and ''A. h. brasilianus'' are year-round residents. Subspecies ''A. h. dabbenei'' vacates the area south from Argentina's [[Neuquén Province]] and adjoining Chile during the [[South|austral]] winter, moving north as far as [[Córdoba Province, Argentina|Córdoba]] and [[Entre Ríos Province|Entre Ríos]] provinces in north-central Argentina. (Note that the map does not show this winter range.)<ref name=HEPI-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The diet of Hellmayr's pipit has not been studied but is known to include larval and adult insects. It forages on the ground while walking or running.<ref name=HEPI-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The breeding season of Hellmayr's pipit varies geographically. In Brazil it spans at least September to April.<ref name=Vasconcelos/> In Bolivia it includes November to January, in Argentina September to February, and in Chile from September to January.<ref name=HEPI-BOW/> Males make a flight display that can reach {{convert|60|m|ft|abbr=on}} above the ground with a spiral descent to the ground. At least in Argentina, both sexes build the nest<ref name=HEPI-BOW/>. The nest is a domed cup made mostly of grass on the ground or attached to low vegetation. The clutch is three to five eggs that are grayish to creamy white with brown or olive-brown spots.<ref name=HEPI-BOW/><ref name=Norambuena>{{cite journal |last1=Rivas |first1=Francisco |last2=Norambuena |first2=Heraldo V. |last3=Grandón Díaz |first3=Catalina |last4=Seitz |first4=Sophie N. |last5=Peña Bello |first5=Esteban |title=Description of the eggs and nest, with notes on the breeding ecology of Hellmayr's Pipit ''Anthus hellmayri dabbenei'' in Chile |journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club |date=4 December 2023 |volume=143 |issue=4 |page=479 |doi=10.25226/bboc.v143i4.2023.a5 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2023BBrOC.143..479R }}</ref> In a study in Argentina incubation lasted 13 to 14 days and fledging occurred nine to 14 days after hatch.<ref name=HEPI-BOW/> Both parents provision nestlings.<ref name=HEPI-BOW/><ref name=Norambuena/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Anthus-hellmayri |species=Hellmayr's pipit}}
===Vocalization===

Hellmayr's pipit sings from a perch, such as a shrub or fence post, and during the display flight. In Brazil its song is a "very/extr. high, short, hurried twitter ending in a nasal drawn-out note, together as ''tritfitwitweét-trutfutwuit-srèèh'', given in 2-3 sec intervals".<ref name=vanPerlo/> A study in Chile's [[Arauco Province]] found its flight song there is "a relatively fast sequence of 3–7 short, complex, high-pitched notes interspersed with a short buzz that becomes longer at the end of the song". When perched it sings a shorter song with shorter buzzes and sometimes just "high-pitched complex warbling notes".<ref name=HEPI-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed Hellmayr's pipit as being of Least Concern. It has a very large range; its population size is not known and is believed to be stable. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is "[l]ocally uncommon to frequent as a breeding species". However, its status in Argentina and Chile is not well understood. "Habitat fragmentation and land cover change are the main threats to this species."<ref name=HEPI-BOW/>
